summary: Jeff Atwood explores the infamous Stack Overflow answer about parsing HTML with regular expressions, which descended into Lovecraftian madness complete with corrupted unicode text invoking Cthulhu. While acknowledging this is a well-understood anti-pattern, he pushes back against absolute dogmatism, arguing that simple string processing tasks don't always require full parsing libraries. The real problem isn't regex itself but programmer ignorance about when each tool is appropriate. He concludes that understanding tools and their trade-offs matters more than blanket prohibitions.
key_points: The viral Stack Overflow answer about HTML parsing became legendary for its Cthulhu-themed descent into unicode madness
Parsing HTML is a solved problem - programmers should be 'lazy' and use existing libraries rather than hand-coding regex
The endless stream of novice developers attempting regex HTML parsing represents an ongoing education problem
Absolute prohibitions against regex for any HTML-related task are as wrongheaded as requiring full parsers for trivial string operations
Understanding the difference between true HTML parsing and simple string processing is the key skill to develop
money_quotes: Every time you attempt to parse HTML with regular expressions, the unholy child weeps the blood of virgins, and Russian hackers pwn your webapp.
Parsing HTML is a solved problem. You do not need to solve it. You just need to be lazy.
The real enemy isn't regular expressions (or, for that matter, goto) but ignorance.
It's more important to understand the tools, and their strengths and weaknesses, than it is to knuckle under to knee-jerk dogmatism.
Unless you have discipline and put very strict conditions on what you're doing, matching HTML with regular expressions rapidly devolves into madness, just how Cthulhu likes it.
We live in a world full of newbie PHP developers doing the first thing that pops into their collective heads, with more born every day.
Whatever method you choose – just don't leave the <cthulhu> tag open, for humanity's sake.
key_insight: The danger of parsing HTML with regex isn't about regex being evil, but about programmers not understanding when simple string processing suffices versus when they actually need a real parser.
